summary: McKenzie shares early transaction data comparing Google Checkout and PayPal adoption among his Bingo Card Creator customers. From 29 transactions, PayPal slightly edged out Google Checkout (16 vs 13), with the notable finding that no Google Checkout users had preexisting accounts while about 60% of PayPal users did. He interprets this as evidence that existing PayPal users actively seek it out when given comparable alternatives. He also notes that his customer demographics (middle-aged women familiar with eBay) likely skew more PayPal-friendly than the tech community.
key_points: PayPal slightly outperformed Google Checkout in raw transaction numbers (16 vs 13 out of 29), despite symmetrical presentation
Zero Google Checkout customers had preexisting accounts, while roughly 60% of PayPal customers did, suggesting PayPal's installed base was a major competitive advantage
Customers with existing PayPal accounts actively seek it out when given comparable alternatives, demonstrating the power of payment habit lock-in
Most customers likely click whichever payment button is in the expected position rather than making a deliberate provider choice
Customer demographics heavily influence payment preferences — his middle-aged female customers were far more PayPal-friendly than the developer community
money_quotes: People who already use Paypal are likely to seek it out as an option when given comparable alternatives.

I suspect many customers are just mashing on any button that they know is in the contextually accurate place for Continue This Transaction Darn It rather than making a conscious decision for or against either provider.
Thats fine by me, incidentally, since I hate having customers have to decide things prior to giving me money.
Many software developers, for example, hate Paypal like the plague for some reason. I think this viewpoint is rather less prevalent among middle aged ladies, who make up a good deal of my customers.
If I had my druthers that order would be randomized for every load of the cart.
key_insight: Payment provider preference is driven far more by existing habits and familiarity than by conscious evaluation, which means reducing friction at checkout matters more than which provider you offer.
